text: 23/6/18, 5*30 pmThere is always tomorrow: Jordan Peterson offers four reasons to choose life Page 1 of 4https://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/inquirer/there-is-always-to…asons-to-choose-life/news-story/b7947b3acdb9cfc8c67f520ed6382570 THE AUSTRALIAN There is always tomorrow: Jordan Peterson offers four reasons to choose life “I plan on taking my own life very soon. Why shouldn’t I?” It was, as the respondent acknowledged, not a casual question. But how to answer, especially in front of a room full of people? That was the conundrum faced by Canadian psychologist Jordan Peterson in Indianapolis on June 15. Those who were there say you could have heard a pin drop. The question, submitted online, had come from a young lad called Chad, who was in the audience. Peterson hesitated before deciding to answer, telling the crowd that he wasn’t sure he could give the question the care and attention it deserved. He has been on tour for months and he was exhausted, but someone’s life was at stake. And also, hasn’t it been on all our minds of late? Suicide, and how to prevent it. Why it happens, and how we might all get to experience vastly less of it. The Australian published a piece about the suicide of fashion designer Kate Spade two weeks ago, just ahead of the news about Anthony Bourdain, and … well, the mail — the questions — just kept coming. Why do people do it? How can we stop them? But also, should we even try? Because that’s an important question, too. What right do we have to try to stop them? From the silence in the auditorium, we can assume, I suppose, that Peterson’s audience — a more optimistic lot than most, probably — also wanted to have the debate. So come on, Mr Philosopher King, what’s your answer? Why should a severely depressed person have to go on ​living? This won’t surprise hard-core fans: Peterson sat cogitating for a while — if you know him, you’ll know the pose, fingers pressed to the forehead — then came up with not one but four reasons for Chad to keep giving life a go. First, anyone contemplating suicide should “think very carefully through the consequences for other people”, Peterson said. “I have clients in my clinical practice who have never recovered from the suicide of a family CAROLINE OVERINGTON THE AUSTRALIAN 12:00AM June 23, 2018 23/6/18, 5*30 pmThere is always tomorrow: Jordan Peterson offers four reasons to choose life Page 2 of 4https://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/inquirer/there-is-always-to…asons-to-choose-life/news-story/b7947b3acdb9cfc8c67f520ed6382570 member. Decades later, they are still torturing themselves about it. Believe me, you will absolutely wipe them out in a way they will never recover from. You cannot fix someone’s suicide. You’re stuck with it. “The problem is, you might be dreaming out that. Maybe you think life is beset against you. Maybe you’re thinking people deserve to suffer for the misery they have imposed upon you. “But I would say: Think very, very carefully before you go down that route, because (suicide) will absolutely devastate, in ways you can’t imagine, the people you leave behind.” So that was reason No 1: other people. Reason No 2: you owe it to yourself to explore all possible ​alternatives before you choose ​suicide. “If you haven’t talked to a psychologist, talked to a psychiatrist, if you haven’t tried antidepressants, if you haven’t revealed to your family that this is how you’re feeling, if you haven’t been to the hospital, well, explore any possible avenue before you take a final step,” Peterson said. Believe it or not, there are treatments for depression, and some of them even work. “For some people, antidepressants work,” Peterson said. “They don’t work for everyone. I’m not claiming they are a panacea, but they certainly beat the hell out of suicide. Even if they have some negative side effects — and they do, quite frequently they do — the negative side effects aren’t fatal. So try that. “You owe it to yourself to try everything else. “A wise man that I once worked for — a psychologist at the maximum-security prison in Edmonton — he used to say: ‘You can always commit suicide tomorrow.’ “That’s a flippant statement, but he meant it in a very serious way. With suicide, you only get to decide once. And you can put it off. So I would say: Put it off, and see what you can do to put yourself ​together. What the hell do you have to lose?” Reason three: consider the possibility that your existence matters in ways that we as human beings, being puny in thought and exiguous in understanding, can’t quite grasp. “Consider that your life has intrinsic value,” Peterson said, meaning you can’t just opt out, because to do so will leave a “hole in the fabric of being itself”. That’s going to be tough for a depressed person, who perhaps can’t see the goodness they’re bringing to the table, but consider it anyway. Then came reason No 4: “Don’t be so sure that your life is yours to take. You don’t own yourself the way you own an object … you can’t just casually bring (your life) to an end because, the fact is, suicide is wrong.” 23/6/18, 5*30 pmThere is always tomorrow: Jordan Peterson offers four reasons to choose life Page 3 of 4https://www.theaustralian.com.au/news/inquirer/there-is-always-to…asons-to-choose-life/news-story/b7947b3acdb9cfc8c67f520ed6382570 June 26, 6pm / June 27, 6pm Brisbane / Sydney Now, you may take umbrage, but on this, if nothing else, all the major faiths agree: it’s morally wrong — no excuses, no exceptions, no further explanation needed — to take your own life. There was a bit more — with Peterson, there’s always a little bit more — but maybe what everyone wants to know is how Chad reacted. Thanks to Twitter, we know the answer, because he used the message service to contact Peterson to express his thanks. His tweet went like this (I’ve kept the typos in, for charm): “Hey Dr Peterson. It’s Chad … I don’t really believe in fate, but I have never felt my adrenaline rush like I did tonight Never in a million years would I expect you to actually get to my question.” He had been feeling cynical, but then it was like he was the only person in the silent auditorium, and it was like Peterson was speaking directly to him, and now Chad had decided to go to hospital, to see what might be done about kicking the black dog to the kerb. To which Peterson replied: “I am absolutely thrilled to hear this.” As will be Chad’s family. It’s a side note, but we received an awful lot of mail after the Spade and Bourdain suicides, some of it from people considering suicide, some of it from people living in the aftermath of a beloved’s suicide, and one message rang clear: if you take your own life, it won’t end the pain. The pain will simply be transferred to other people, who then have to live their lives in agony. So, again, don’t do it. Try everything else first, and then stop yourself one more time. Lifeline: 13 11 14. Overingtonc@theaustralian .com.au Q&A CRACKING A COLD CASE Discuss our latest podcast- 'The Teacher's Pet' with award winning journalist Hedley Thomas.
